coredata.dc:title: Adding carbon quantum dots to enhance microbial extracellular electron transfer for accelerating aniline degradation in simulated wetlands

coredata.dc:description: Microbial extracellular respiration is relatively weak in natural water systems because of the low electroactivity of environmental media, which limits the production of extracellular respiration-based reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the removal of pollutant. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were added to a simulated wetland to enhance extracellular respiration and accelerate the microbial oxidation of aniline under intermittent aeration conditions. The results indicated that CDs promoted extracellular electron transfer to increase the production of Fe2+ and ROS, and led to a 23.5 % higher aniline removal rate than that of the control group without CD addition. Multiple lines of evidence indicated that CDs bind to microorganisms in wetlands to induce the polarization of respiratory enzymes, thereby enhancing microbial electroactivity and the activity of respiratory chain enzymes. In addition, CDs enhanced proton efflux to form a higher membrane potential for adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production, which facilitated ROS generation. This study demonstrated a simple and efficient approach to enhance microbial extracellular electron transfer for ROS production and remediation of polluted wetlands.

head.ce:title: Adding carbon quantum dots to enhance microbial extracellular electron transfer for accelerating aniline degradation in simulated wetlands

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (Dalian University of Technology), Ministry of Education, School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial extracellular respiration is relatively weak in natural water systems because of the low electroactivity of environmental media, which limits the production of extracellular respiration-based reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the removal of pollutant. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were added to a simulated wetland to enhance extracellular respiration and accelerate the microbial oxidation of aniline under intermittent aeration conditions. The results indicated that CDs promoted extracellular electron transfer to increase the production of Fe2+ and ROS, and led to a 23.5 % higher aniline removal rate than that of the control group without CD addition. Multiple lines of evidence indicated that CDs bind to microorganisms in wetlands to induce the polarization of respiratory enzymes, thereby enhancing microbial electroactivity and the activity of respiratory chain enzymes. In addition, CDs enhanced proton efflux to form a higher membrane potential for adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production, which facilitated ROS generation. This study demonstrated a simple and efficient approach to enhance microbial extracellular electron transfer for ROS production and remediation of polluted wetlands.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are widely present in natural systems and play a crucial role in biogeochemical processes such as pollutant degradation (Yuan et al., 2018) and elemental cycling (Liu et al., 2020; Zhang and Yuan, 2017). ROS are typically produced via photochemical reactions (Diaz et al., 2013). Recently, it has been reported that ROS may be generated in redox oscillation environments, where anaerobic and aerobic conditions alternate (Zhao et al., 2023; Dai et al., 2022), such as in paddy fields, coastal zones, and artificial ecosystems (Liu et al., 2024a). Even under dark conditions, electrons generated by microbial respiration can be transferred to extracellular iron minerals or O2 to form Fenton-like reactions and produce hydroxyl radicals (•OH) (Liu et al., 2024b). In activated sludge, redox oscillations trigger ROS generation to enhance organic micropollutant degradation (Liu et al., 2024c). Similarly, in soil systems, ROS production is indirectly regulated by temperature and soil moisture through root exudate release (e.g., phenolics), while oxygen availability directly mediates ROS production by serving as a precursor (Liu et al., 2024d). Furthermore, groundwater table fluctuations (GTF) significantly influence ROS dynamics (Wei et al., 2024).

Specifically, under anaerobic conditions, iron minerals act as extracellular electron acceptors, receiving electrons from exoelectrogens via dissimilatory iron reduction to generate Fe2+ (Tong et al., 2015). Under aerobic conditions, O2 may receive electrons from exoelectrogen metabolism to generate superoxide anions (O2·-) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Dai et al., 2022). Thus, extracellular H2O2 and Fe2+ generate •OH via a Fenton-like reaction to degrade contaminants (Han et al., 2019).

Therefore, intermittent aeration by artificially creating redox oscillating environments is likely to promote ROS production. He et al. found that alternating redox environments generated large amounts of ROS to regulate the biogeochemical Fe/S/C cycle for contaminated paddy soil remediation (He et al., 2024). Wang et al. controlled the dissolved oxygen (DO) level to adjust the generation of ROS and the removal of pollutants (Wang et al., 2023). Compared to ROS generation through chemical and physical methods, such as light, electricity, and heat, intermittent aeration is cost-effective and environmentally friendly, offering a novel approach for pollutant removal in in water environments such as wetlands (Yan et al., 2016). However, microbial extracellular respiration typically does not dominate in wetlands (Zhang et al., 2024a,b) because of the low conductivity of the sediment, leading to inefficient pollutant removal via this microbial-based oxidation reaction.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), which are low-cost conductive carbon nanomaterials with high electron transfer efficiency, biocompatibility, and stability (Kausar, 2019; Ren et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2021a), have the potential to enhance microbial extracellular respiration (Yang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2024b). Compared to metal-based nanomaterials, CDs exhibit superior biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2020). Their potential interactions with intracellular cytochromes/enzymes may modulate cellular redox reactions, thereby regulating metabolic activity. CDs synthesized from natural precursors (e.g., citric acid, biomass) demonstrate partial biodegradability, which helps minimize environmental persistence (Meng et al., 2019). Furthermore, CDs are considered a safer option in terms of secondary pollution, as they exhibit minimal leaching of toxic ions. To date, CDs have demonstrated excellent performance in photo/electrocatalysis (Chen et al., 2017) and biological hybridization (Lin et al., 2015), however, there are few studies on augmenting ROS production and pollutant removal by CDs to enhance microbial extracellular electron transfer.

In this study, intermittent aeration was used to simulate redox oscillations in wetlands, and CDs were added to enhance extracellular electron transfer and accelerate microbial-based pollutant removal. Aniline was used as the target pollutant since it is recalcitrant to degradation and prone to accumulate in soil and water bodies (Yin et al., 2023). ROS production, Fe changes, and DO effects were investigated. In addition, functional group of the microbial aggregates and electron transfer chain were analyzed.

Citric acid (0.5 g) was placed in a 5 mL beaker (Φ2.0 × 5 cm) and heated to 200 °C in an oil bath pot filled with phenylmethyl silicone fluid. This process was continued until the citric acid melted and transitioned from yellow to dark red liquid. The liquid was then added to a 10 g/L NaOH solution and the mixture was stirred for 10 min until a reddish-brown solution was obtained without any precipitate. The obtained solution was dialyzed for 24 h at 25 °C with the molecular weight of 1 KD as the lower limit. Water-soluble CDs were then obtained by cooling.

The sediment used in this study was collected from a wetland park in Henan, China. To ensure representativeness, sediment samples were collected from 10 different locations in this wetland. After drying, grinding, and sieving, the sediments were placed in experimental bottles and activated using synthetic glucose-based wastewater (Table S1). The primary characteristics of the sediments were as follows: total polysaccharides, 977.78 ± 81.98 mg/L; total proteins, 708.17 ± 45.22 mg/L; total suspended solids, 17973 ± 561 mg/kg sediment; and volatile suspended solids, 38,197 ± 836 mg/kg sediment.

Six culture bottles with a working volume of 500 mL (Φ8.6 × 18.2 cm) were used. Three bottles supplemented with 10 mg of the prepared CDs were referred to as the CD group, whereas the remaining three bottles that did not receive CDs were referred to as the control group. Each bottle received 50 g of activated sediment and 350 mL of synthetic wastewater (Table S1) and was incubated at 30 °C in a constant-temperature incubator. The bottles were intermittently aerated using an air pump (1 L/min) to simulate the redox oscillations in the wetlands. Bottles were aerated for 12 h daily to create aerobic conditions, and aeration was halted for the remaining 12 h daily to create anaerobic conditions.

The experiment consisted of three phases with an influent aniline concentration of 150 mg/L. When aniline was depleted, 300 mL of the supernatant was carefully withdrawn from the bottle using a syringe, and an equal volume of aniline-containing feed was added. Aniline concentrations were determined using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC; Shimadzu LC-2050 C). Before analysis, 2 mL of supernatant was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane.

ROS production was analyzed qualitatively and quantitatively. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was used to qualitatively analyze ROS production in situ. Next, 10 mL of 5,5 dimethylpyrrolidine-n-oxide (DMPO) solution (100 mM) was added to 2 g sediments of the CD and control groups for •OH analysis (Huang et al., 2021). For O2 ·- analysis, 10 mL of dimethylsulphoxide (DMSO) was added to 2.0 g sediments of the CD and control groups, and then 10 mL of DMPO (100 mM) prepared in DMSO was added as the O2 ·- trapping agent (Zhao et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2019). The samples were analyzed using a Bruker EMXplus EPR spectrometer.

Sodium benzoate (BA) was used as a chemical probe to quantitatively detect •OH generation (Niyuhire et al., 2019). The cumulative •OH concentration was determined by the formation of p-HBA (C•OH = 5.87 × Cp-HBA) upon BA hydroxylation by reaction with •OH. A mixture was prepared by combining 50 μL of the suspension with 5 mL of 10 mM BA solution and incubating it in the dark for 1 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 8000×g for 5 min to collect the supernatant. The supernatant was filtered using a syringe equipped with a 0.22 μm membrane. The p-HBA concentration was analyzed by HPLC (Shimadzu, LC-2050 C) with a UV detector set at 255 nm and an InertSustain C18 column. The mobile phase for HPLC consisted of 85 % methanol and 15 % ultrapure water, with a flow rate of 1 mL min−1. H2O2 levels were measured using the N,N-diethyl-P-phenylenediamine (DPD) method (Zou et al., 2019). O2·- production was detected using 2,3-bis (2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulphophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide (XTT) as a probe (Fu et al., 2016). The detailed test methods for O2·- and H2O2 are described in the Supporting Information (Text 1).

Electrochemical in situ FTIR was performed using a Bruker VERTEX 70 to analyze the electrical signals of the CD group compared with those of the control group sediments, focusing on changes in the functional groups of microbial proteins (Yu et al., 2015). A 200 μL suspension of both groups of sediments after washing three times with 0.01 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was mixed with 1 % Nafion water solution (2.5 %, v:v) and uniformly coated onto a Pt electrode, followed by drying in an infrared drying oven. The coated Pt, Ag/AgCl reference, and Pt counter electrodes served as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. An electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, CHI 1030 C) was employed with a working potential ranging from −0.90 V to 0.40 V.

Thioflavin T (ThT), a fluorescent cationic dye, has been used to qualitatively observe the membrane potential within biological membranes (Prindle et al., 2015). Owing to the positive charge of ThT, a more negative intracellular membrane potential may result in greater ThT retention in the cell, resulting in a deeper blue-green color (Fig. S1). A 1 mL suspension from the CD and control group sediments was uniformly applied to a microfluidic cell culture chip (Fluigent FLOW-EZ). ThT (at a 10 mM concentration and a 1 μL/min flow rate) was introduced from the opposite side of the chip using a liquid pump to mix with the suspensions. Real-time observation and continuous imaging were performed using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX 83).

The morphology of the citrate-synthesized CDs was characterized by field transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 , the synthesized CDs were spherical with an average diameter of <5 nm. Both individual CD granules (Fig. 1C) and aggregated CDs (Fig. 1A and B) exhibited distinct lattice structures, indicating that CDs were successfully produced using sodium citrate as the precursor. Nano CDs may interact with microorganisms, potentially facilitating electron transfer from the intracellular to the extracellular space, thereby enhancing microbial metabolism (Guan et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs accelerated aniline removal under intermittent aeration
ce:section.ce:para: To simulate the redox fluctuations in the wetland, an intermittent aeration strategy was employed, providing air for 12 h daily to investigate the effect of CDs on aniline degradation (Ilyas and Masih, 2017). Aniline at 150 mg/L concentration was added to the CD and control (CD-free) groups, and after complete degradation, further aniline was added. The experiment was conducted in three cycles, each lasting for eight days. As shown in Fig. 2 , the addition of CDs significantly enhanced aniline removal under intermittent aeration conditions in the simulated wetland. For example, on day 13, the aniline concentration in the CD group was 26.75 mg/L, compared to 62.01 mg/L in the control group, which indicates a 23.5 % higher degradation rate for the CD group. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) (Table S3, Text 7) for the three change cycles in aniline concentrations showed that the differences were significant (p < 0.05), indicating that the CDs accelerated aniline degradation in the simulated wetlands.

A sterilization test showed the addition of CDs caused negligible removal of aniline under anaerobic conditions which showed that adsorption, volatilization did not cause significant removal of aniline (Fig. S2). This indicated that microbial metabolism was necessary for the CDs to enhance aniline degradation in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs accelerated ROS production under intermittent aeration
ce:section.ce:para:
Under aerobic conditions, organic matter was microbially oxidized to produce intracellular electrons, with oxygen as the electron acceptor (Herst et al., 2004). In this study, oxygen was abundant in the aerobic stage of continuous aeration, although electrons were relatively scarce, leading to oxygen reduction to produce O2 ·- via a one-electron pathway (reaction 1). Then, O2 ·- could acquire electrons to form H2O2 (reaction 2), which subsequently generated •OH through the Fenton-like reaction with Fe2+ as the electron donor (reaction 3) (Han et al., 2019).
O2 + e− → O2 ·- (reaction 1)
2O2 ·- + 2H+ → H2O2 + O2 (reaction 2)
Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH− + •OH (reaction 3)
Under anaerobic conditions, the lack of oxygen results in a decrease in the ROS concentration. Consequently, the ROS concentration fluctuated under intermittent aeration conditions.

Notably, the ROS concentrations in the CD group were higher than those in the control group during the anaerobic and aerobic stages. For example, after halting 12 h aeration at 48 h, the O2 ·- concentration in the CD group was 0.36 μmol/L, which was 153.85 % higher than that in the control group (0.14 μmol/L). The H2O2 was 4.97 μmol/L in the CD group, which was 120.27 % higher than that in the control group (2.26 μmol/L), and the •OH was 12.69 μmol/L, which was 6.64 % higher than that in the control group (11.90 μmol/L). Upon termination of the 12 h continuous aeration stage (at 60 h), the O2 ·- concentration in the CD group was 0.87 μmol/L, which was 51.43 % higher than that in the control group (0.57 μmol/L). The H2O2 was 6.91 μmol/L in the CD group, which was 67.80 % higher than that in the control group (4.12 μmol/L), and the •OH was 46.47 μmol/L, which was 19.55 % higher than that in the control group (38.87 μmol/L). Consistently, EPR spectra showed significantly stronger signals for •OH (Fig. S3A) and O2 ·- (Fig. S3B) in the CD group compared to those in the control group. It is well known that pollutant degradation in microbial advanced oxidation processes primarily relies on ROS, particularly •OH (Li et al., 2022). This suggests that CDs addition accelerated extracellular ROS generation by enhancing microbial extracellular electron transfer, leading to an increase in O2 ·- and H2O2 production, which promoted •OH production to accelerate aniline degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs accelerated dissimilatory iron reduction under intermittent aeration
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3D, the trend in the Fe2+ concentration was similar to that of ROS, with both exhibiting sawtooth-like fluctuations. However, the total Fe2+ concentration increased under anaerobic conditions during the 12 h aeration cessation and decreased under aerobic conditions during the 12 h continuous aeration, in contrast to that of the ROS. Under aerobic conditions, Fe2+ was oxidized by oxygen to Fe3+, which reduced the total Fe2+ concentration, whereas the ROS increased. Conversely, under anaerobic conditions, Fe3+, as an extracellular electron acceptor, was reduced to Fe2+ through dissimilatory iron reduction (Zhu et al., 2018), which increased the Fe2+ concentration, whereas the ROS concentration decreased owing to the lack of oxygen.

Notably, the total Fe2+ concentration in the CD group was higher than that in the control group during aerobic and anaerobic stages. Under anaerobic conditions with no aeration at 24 h, the total Fe2+ concentration in the CD group was 68.92 mg/L, which was 18.32 % higher than that of the control group (58.25 mg/L). Under aerobic conditions with aeration at 60 h, the total Fe2+ concentration in the CD group was 54.03 mg/L, which was 21.26 % higher than that in the control group (44.56 mg/L).

This further indicated that CDs accelerated extracellular electron transfers of microorganisms, thereby enhancing dissimilatory iron reduction and resulting in a higher total Fe2+ concentration in the CD group than in the control group. The increase in total Fe2+ promoted O2 ·- and H2O2 formation (reactions 4 and 5), which generated •OH and consequently accelerated aniline degradation (Han et al., 2019).
Fe2+ + O2 → Fe3+ + O2 ·- (reaction 4)
Fe2+ + O2 ·- + 2H+ → Fe3+ + H2O2 (reaction 5)

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs accelerated Fenton-like reactions under intermittent aeration
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the effect of CDs on extracellular electron transfer and ROS generation, the DO, ROS, and Fe2+ contents were continuously measured after 12 h of aeration and 12 h of aeration cessation.

Under aerobic and anaerobic conditions, the DO content in the CD group was lower than that in the control group (Fig. S4). Notably, the average DO of the control group during the anaerobic stage was 0.39 mg/L, which was 178.6 % higher than that of the CD group (0.14 ppm). The lower DO of the CD group might be attributed to the CDs accelerating electron transfer to enhance oxygen reduction to ROS, which decreased DO (Zhao et al., 2022).

COD concentrations in the simulated wetlands were measured (Fig. S5). Under both aeration and aeration cessation conditions, the COD degradation rate in the CD group was significantly higher than that in the control group. Notably, after 6 h of aeration, the COD in the CD group decreased from 3000 mg/L in the influent to 97.82 mg/L, which was lower than that in the control group (321.07 mg/L). Faster COD removal in the CD group indicated that the CDs accelerated the utilization of COD as an electron donor, thereby generating additional ROS.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the ROS concentration under aerobic conditions was higher than that under anaerobic conditions, with an increasing trend over time during the aerobic stage and a decreasing trend during the anaerobic stage. In contrast, the Fe2+ concentration in the supernatant was lower under aerobic conditions than under anaerobic conditions, and it decreased over time during the aerobic stage, while increasing over time during anaerobic conditions. Under aeration, the ample DO gradually oxidized Fe2+, and the generated extracellular H2O2 and other ROS participated in Fenton-like reactions with Fe2+, which reduced its concentration and produced •OH. After aeration cessation, Fe3+, acting as the primary extracellular electron acceptor, was reduced to Fe2+, leading to a gradual increase in the Fe2+ concentration, which reached its highest level upon termination of the anaerobic stage.

Notably, the ROS concentration in the CD group was significantly higher than that in the control group under both anaerobic and aerobic conditions. For example, at 5 h during the aerobic stage, the H2O2 concentration in the CD group (8.54 μmol/L) was 161.52 % higher than that in the control group (3.26 μmol/L). Concurrently, the O2 ·- concentration in the CD group (0.54 μmol/L) was 19.28 % higher than that in the control group (0.45 μmol/L), and the •OH concentration in the CD group (41.55 μmol/L) was 13.00 % higher than that in the control group (36.77 μmol/L). During the anaerobic stage, for example, at 20 h, the H2O2 concentration in the CD group (4.13 μmol/L) was 277.53 % higher than that in the control group (1.09 μmol/L). At this time, the O2 ·- concentration in the CD group (0.32 μmol/L) was 56.76 % higher than that in the control group (0.20 μmol/L), and the •OH concentration in the CD group (13.73 μmol/L) was 9.72 % higher than that in the control group (12.51 μmol/L).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy showed that CDs addition increased the proportion of Fe2+ in the total iron content of the sediments (Fig. S6). In the aerobic and anaerobic stages, the Fe2+ concentration in the supernatant of the CD group was higher than that in the control group, which was conducive to the occurrence of Fenton-like reactions. For example, at 8 h in the aerobic stage, the Fe2+ concentration in the CD group was 0.28 mg/L, which was 31.49 % higher than that in the control group (0.21 mg/L); and at 18 h in the anaerobic stage, it was 0.40 mg/L, which was 31.15 % higher than that in the control group (0.31 mg/L). This is consistent with previous studies, where an increased Fe2+ concentration typically led to ROS generation, and Fe2+ participated in their formation (reactions 4 and 5). Thus, the accelerated dissimilatory iron reduction in the CD group resulted in faster ROS generation, facilitating the occurrence of Fenton-like reactions.

These results indicate that CDs significantly accelerated ROS production in the system. ROS served as critical signaling molecules in biological systems, regulating antioxidant defense mechanisms (Lennicke and Cocheme, 2021). When exposed to low levels of ROS, microorganisms could activate the expression of antioxidant enzymes (e.g., superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px)), thereby enhancing their oxidative stress tolerance. •OH, a typical ROS, plays a crucial role in pollutant degradation. In the aqueous phase, •OH is primarily generated via Fenton-like reactions (Qin et al., 2020). Under anaerobic and aerobic conditions, the CD group exhibited significantly higher levels of H2O2 and Fe2+ in the supernatant than those in the control group. This suggests that CDs accelerated the microbial uptake of organic matter and the utilization of electron donors, thus accelerating extracellular electron transfer and ROS and Fe2+ generation, which in turn enhanced the rate of Fenton-like reactions. Fe2+ was generated by dissimilatory iron reduction via extracellular electron transfer under anaerobic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs enhanced the electroactivity of microbial communities
ce:section.ce:para: From the cyclic voltammetry (CV) I-V curve (Fig. S7A), the CD group capacitance was 1.748 × 10−4 F, which was 129.9 % higher than that of the control group (7.606 × 10−5 F). This increased capacitance provided additional storage sites for extracellular electron transfer (Wang et al., 2020). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. S7B) indicated that CDs addition reduced the resistance of the wetland sediments. Specifically, the resistance of the CD group was 48.35 Ω, which was 12.68 % lower than that of the control group (55.37 Ω). A reduction in resistance enhances the extracellular electron transfer capabilities of microorganisms (Yu and Zhang, 2019), thereby accelerating dissimilatory iron reduction and ROS generation.

CDs addition increased the abundance of electroactive microorganisms (Text 4, Fig. S8). Specifically, bacteria with extracellular respiratory capabilities, such as Brevundimonas (Rimboud et al., 2017) and Desulfobulbus (Chabert et al., 2015), were enriched under the influence of CDs, and their abundances increased 32.17- and 1.38-fold, respectively, compared to those of the control group. Notably, the abundance of Pseudomonas increased by 120.9 % (1.85 % versus 0.84 %). According to previous studies, Pseudomonas can directly mediate iron reduction by secreting acidic and reducing metabolites (Zheng et al., 2024), which is consistent with the Fe2+ concentration in the CD group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polarization of amide groups induced by CDs facilitates electron transfer in microorganisms
ce:section.ce:para: In-situ FTIR was used to identify polarity changes in the microbial functional groups in sediments. In the sludge sample with CDs added, as the test potential increased from −0.9 V to 0.4 V (versus Ag/AgCl), significant changes were observed in the absorption peaks at 1550 and 1650 cm−1, which corresponded to C-N and C=O groups, respectively (Yu et al., 2015). In contrast, sludge samples without CDs exhibited minimal changes (Fig. 5 A). Given that C=O and C-N are the fundamental structural units of protein amino acids (amide groups) (Niki et al., 2003), the substantial increase in the infrared absorption band intensity of C=O and C-N indicated that the protein complexes may undergo polarization after electrical stimulation (Liu et al., 2021). The polarized amide groups indicated that protein-like substances could provide additional electron storage sites during microbial electron transfer (Dubois et al., 2004), which agreed with the higher capacitance of the CD group in the CV tests.

However, polarization of the C=O amide groups pushed electrons away from the oxygen atoms, which facilitated C−O−H formation through the acceptance of protons or hydrogen radicals released from surrounding hydrogen bonds (N-H) following their dissociation (Sawicka et al., 2003). This process participated in proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) in protein-like substances (respiratory enzymes) through hydrogen bond reorganization (Chen et al., 2014). This enhanced the electron transfer of microorganisms in the sediments, which was consistent with the lower resistance results of EIS in the CD group.

PCET is a common mode of electron transfer in the electron transportation chain during microbial extracellular respiration, and occurs in processes such as NADH oxidation and the Q cycle in Complex I (Saura and Kaila, 2019). PCET in respiratory enzymes facilitates microbial metabolism (electron transfer) and energy conversion (proton output for adenosine triphosphate (ATP) generation) (Courbon and Rubinstein, 2022).

Kinetic isotopic effect (KIE) experiments were used to verify the electron transfer mode of microorganisms in sediments under CD-induced polarization (Okamoto et al., 2017) (Fig. 5B, Text 5). Before the addition of heavy water (D2O), the initial current of the CD group was 2.35 × 10−5 A, which was 1.36-times that of the control group (1.72 × 10−5 A). This confirmed that CDs addition reduced the resistance of the sediments and increased their conductivity. After D2O addition, the current initially increased sharply owing to the fluid disturbance caused by the D2O but then began to decrease continuously. Eventually, the current stabilized at approximately 1.14 × 10−5 A, showing a 13.12 % higher decrease than that of the current in the control group (1.06 × 10−5 A). The current ratio (
I H
/
I D
) in the CD group before and after D2O addition was 2.061, which was higher than the corresponding value in the control group (
I H
/
I D
= 1.623).

Compared to H, D is more difficult to activate because of its greater mass. When D2O was added to the solvent, D replaced the H in H2O, slowing the reaction in which H participates (Okamoto et al., 2017). When electron transfer is coupled with proton-related reactions, D2O addition delayed electron transfer, resulting in a lower current (Yu et al., 2023). This phenomenon during PCET is known as the KIE. The more pronounced decrease in current in the CD group indicated that electron transfer was more significantly affected by the mass of atoms, suggesting that PCET played a dominant role in microorganisms. This was attributed to polarization of the amide groups induced by the CDs, which facilitated hydrogen bond rearrangement and promoted the transfer of protons and electrons during microbial extracellular respiration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs accelerated electron transfer in the respiratory chain
ce:section.ce:para: To determine whether CDs influenced electron transfer in the respiratory chain, the content of coenzymes involved in electron exchange with the respiratory chain and the activity of respiratory enzymes were measured (Table S2, Text 6). As shown in Fig. 6 , the NAD+/NADH ratio in the CD group was 1.651, higher than that in the control group (1.403). Coenzyme NADH stores electrons produced from the oxidation of organic matter and donates them to respiratory enzymes in the respiratory chain to form NAD+ (Saura and Kaila, 2019). A higher NAD+/NADH ratio indicated a faster NADH conversion to NAD+, which resulted from enhanced intracellular electron mobility with CDs.

Simultaneously, during microbial respiration, the activity of respiratory enzyme complexes I and III, which are involved in electron transfer and proton coupling (Lapuente-Brun et al., 2013), were significantly enhanced. In the CD group, the activity of Complex I was 261.57 ± 1.58 IU/L, an 18.83 % increase compared to that in the control group (220.12 ± 1.20 IU/L). The activity of Complex III in the CD group was 499.3 ± 0.80 IU/L, a 4.16 % increase compared to that in the control group (479.3 ± 1.11 IU/L). In Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), the increase of the enzyme activity promoted the NADH oxidation and electron transfer through FMN/Fe-S clusters, driving proton pumping (4H+/2e−) via conformational changes in the membrane (Vinogradov, 2008); while in Complex III (cytochrome bc1 complex), the increase of the enzyme activity facilitated the ubiquinol oxidation and Q-cycle turnover to accelerate the electron transfer to cytochrome c/heme bL, which enhanced the coupled proton translocation (2H+/e−) (Xia et al., 2013). In addition, the ATP/ADP ratio in the CD group was 0.163, which was higher than that in the control group (0.148), indicating abundant intracellular energy generation, which benefited microbial metabolism (Man et al., 2020).

The accelerated outflow of electrons and protons in the respiratory chain led to changes in membrane potential. To investigate the impact of CDs on the membrane potential, the sediments were stained with ThT, a fluorescent dye. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of the stained regions in the CD group was stronger, indicating that CDs addition accelerated proton outflow in the respiratory chain. This is because ThT, positively charged, exhibited electrostatic binding with negatively-charged microbial cells. The degree of ThT binding correlated with membrane potential hyperpolarization - as the negative potential enhanced the ThT accumulation. The increase in proton outflow indicated that the internal membrane potential was more negative, resulting in additional ThT being retained in the cell to increase the fluorescence intensity (Liu et al., 2015). According to the PCET, proton outflow enhancement may be accompanied by the accelerated motion of electrons to increase ROS generation. More importantly, the enhanced proton outflow in the respiratory chain generated a higher H+ gradient (proton motive force), which further promoted ATP production to promote the growth of the related microorganisms (Courbon and Rubinstein, 2022).

Recently, it has been found that ROS generated from microbial extracellular respiration play an important role in the biogeochemical cycle of redox oscillation environments, such as wetlands, tidal zones, and plant rhizospheres (Liu et al., 2024b; Tong et al., 2015). This Fenton-like reaction depends on extracellular electron transfer. Specifically, the maximal export of intercellular electrons to the exterior is required to reduce O2 to H2O2 under aerobic conditions or to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ under anaerobic conditions. However, the electrical inertness of aquatic environment sediments is detrimental to this process (Zhao et al., 2023). Electrochemical methods have been used to improve the electroactivity of sediments via electrode implantation (Song et al., 2024), however, they are energy-intensive and complex to establish (Gupta et al., 2021).

This study is the first to add CDs to mixed cultures such as wetlands to accelerate microbial extracellular respiration and pollutant removal via ROS generation. Comparatively, this method saves energy and is simple. CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and environmental safety. CDs have been reported to have little effect on various organisms. Even if the CDs concentration is as high as 400 mg/L, the organisms can still maintain >90 % activity (Qin et al., 2021). The amount of CDs added in this study was relatively low (25 mg/L), much lower than that reported in the literature. The results of this study showed that CDs induced polarization of microbial proteins (amide groups) to enhance microbial electroactivity and electron transfer. In addition, CDs addition accelerated PCET to drive proton outflow, which increased membrane potential and ATP production.

Furthermore, biochar derived from the incomplete combustion of natural materials under oxygen-deficient conditions may enhance microbial advanced oxidation (Wu et al., 2021b) in fluctuating redox zones. Biochar with electroactivity and a high surface area can serve as an electron bridge to establish long-distance electron connections between microorganisms attached to its surface and extracellular electron acceptors, thereby enhancing extracellular respiration and pollutant decomposition via microbial Fenton-like reactions (Yu et al., 2016). The impact of biochar on microbial advanced oxidation will be investigated in future studies.

This study is the first to report that in redox oscillating environments such as wetlands, the addition of CDs could enhance pollutant removal by promoting microbial respiration and ROS production. The results showed that CDs enhanced microbial electrical activity and electron transfer, which induced Fe3+ reduction and Fe2+ production under anaerobic conditions through extracellular electron transfer, or induced O2 reduction to O2 ·- and H2O2 under aerobic conditions. This led to a Fenton-like reaction to remove aniline from the wetlands. This study provided a new strategy for in-situ remediation of wetland refractory pollutants.

Xingyuan Song: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Ruijia Yang: Data curation. Junwu Yi: Data curation. Ziyang Zhang: Methodology. Haohao Mao: Methodology. Qilin Yu: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhiqiang Zhao: Conceptualization. Yaobin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
